categories.title: ElizaOS Community Discussion and Development Updates

content.text: In the general discussion channel, community members exchanged casual greetings throughout the day. A user named ross inquired about whether orders were still being taken for humanoid robots, referencing a news article with a dead link mentioning an amount of 420,000. Odilitime responded that the idea had been started but fell apart. A developer named Ginga asked if anyone was looking to hire a developer. Inhuman Resources shared a Web3 trivia game post promoting an online learning platform with multiple categories, describing it as a safe and fun space for everyone. Ross also shared an episode of the Cron Job series titled The Orchestrator Awakens covering the week of May 31 to June 6, 2026, which highlighted ElizaOS launching a coding workbench achieving parity with Claude Code and Codex, a production outage affecting Eliza Cloud login, the decomposition of the monolithic plugin-lifeops into seven modular plugins, and a surge of DeFAI builders joining the ecosystem.

In the coders channel, activity was minimal with community members Solify and bobjonesgood exchanging brief greetings. The channel topic directs users to the official ElizaOS documentation at docs.elizaos.ai to get started with building agents.

A community member named Bawss raised a debate about whether elizaOS should remain fully open source in 2026, arguing that the AI landscape has become highly competitive and secretive. He suggested the team should consider monetizing their tools and potentially only open-sourcing older versions of their software, similar to how Elon Musk handled xAI. He expressed concern that larger companies could replicate their work faster and that the project appeared to lack clear direction and resources, referencing past issues related to the a16z migration and alleged loss of funds. Community members pushed back on this view. IVgamer pointed out that open source does not necessarily mean losing competitive advantage, as being the foundation of a technology still holds value, and noted that a commercialization partnership between Secure Blockchain Development Corp. and the Eliza Foundation already exists for enterprise-level AI agents built on the elizaOS framework. Ross added that providing free access to knowledge is a form of equity and is never aimless. Bawss acknowledged he lacked full understanding of these developments and asked for clarification on the commercialization efforts.

A user named Ryan reported missing the deadline to migrate legacy AI16Z tokens to ELIZAOS, with the tokens sitting untouched in a private Coinbase wallet. He asked whether any alternative process, smart contract pool, or manual appeal existed for holders who missed the migration cutoff, or whether the window was strictly finalized with no fallback option.

Spam and scam activity was noted across both the general discussion and coders channels. Multiple messages were flagged as scams, including a user named Josh repeatedly posting offers to buy old Solana wallets with transaction history for amounts ranging from 0.5 SOL to 2 SOL or more depending on wallet age. Another user posted a fake airdrop message claiming a 500 dollar prize. A mini moderator named Omid Sa flagged the issue of scammer tagging, noting that the dedicated mini mod channel no longer exists, making it necessary to tag scammers in main channels. Odilitime confirmed granting Omid Sa mini mod powers to help address this. A warning video was also shared in the discussion channel related to scam awareness.

Inhuman Resources shared a trivia post from X asking which Allied nation suffered the highest total military and civilian casualties in World War II, with answer options including the United States, Great Britain, the Soviet Union, and China. A second trivia question was also shared asking which 1992 holiday movie featured a cameo by Donald Trump at the Plaza Hotel. Community members also briefly discussed the Federal Reserve interest rate decision, with one member predicting no change.

categories.title: ElizaOS Community Discussion: Open Source Philosophy, Accessibility Concerns, Lawsuit Claims, and Platform Status

content.text: A core developer shared their perspective on open source software, arguing that innovators are worth more than their intellectual property, that software is no longer a competitive moat in the AI age, that marketing and user acquisition are more costly than development, and that operating from a place of confidence and generosity is preferable to fear-based secrecy. The developer also clarified that ElizaOS is not purely altruistic like early OpenAI, but is an organization that aims to make money primarily through its cloud platform while also pursuing goals of democratizing AI and keeping it accessible to the general public.

A long-time community member who has been present since December 2024 raised strong concerns about the accessibility and communication around ElizaOS products. They expressed frustration that after over a year, they still do not clearly understand what Eliza Cloud is or how the broader organization works. They pointed out that the cloud website lacks an about page, FAQs, and clear explanations for general users. They strongly advocated for a no-code, click-based interface that would allow non-developers to easily create, deploy, and manage agents, suggesting a simple login flow with social media integrations and agent customization options. They also suggested that the product branding of ElizaOS as an operating system for AI agents would be more intuitive and marketable than the current Eliza Cloud branding, which they said is associated with storage rather than agent deployment. Another community member responded that Eliza Cloud does in fact allow users to create, modify, and deploy agents without coding experience.

A community member raised a question about a reported lawsuit filed against ElizaOS members alleging manipulation and fake decentralized identity. Another user shared a link to the ElizaOS X account and referenced a claim depot article describing an AI16Z class action lawsuit alleging that founders faked an AI agent and misled investors in a scheme valued at approximately 2.6 billion dollars. The article cited that a new ELIZA token was released on November 19, 2024, surged past an 80 million dollar market cap within one hour, while the original token crashed approximately 87 percent. The lawsuit also cited on-chain data allegedly showing a wallet linked to an AI16Z managing partner sold holdings immediately before a public announcement, characterizing it as insider trading. Some community members dismissed the claims as FUD, while others acknowledged the situation and noted they view the project differently.

A user reported that Eliza Cloud came back online and they were able to log in, but noted issues including being unable to scroll down on the interface and being unable to connect a Milady account to the cloud platform.
In the coders channel, there was brief hostile exchange between two users, with one user directing insults at another. A separate user posted a greeting in Russian.
No activity was recorded for the date of 2026-06-18. There is no data or events to report for this period.

categories.title: ElizaOS Community Discussion: Marketing, Project Updates, and Concerns

content.text: In the general discussion channel, community members raised several topics on June 19, 2026. One user offered marketing advice directed at the ElizaOS team, suggesting the project should position itself as 'the operating system for AI agents' under the branding 'Eliza OS' rather than 'Eliza Cloud,' arguing that consumer-friendly branding and ease of use are critical for mainstream adoption. The user also praised the existing Eliza mascot as strong visual marketing. Other members noted the full product has not yet launched and that the team is still actively building and upgrading the project.

A community member posted a detailed fact-check regarding ElizaOS and a company called SecureBlockchain Dev Corp. According to the post, a Canadian micro-cap company rebranded into SecureBlockchain Dev Corp in October 2025, then acquired a company called Agentric Solutions in February 2026, co-founded by Sebastian Quinn Watson, who is named as a defendant in a current New York legal case. In April 2026, the company raised 1.5 million CAD, with the Eliza Foundation reportedly allocating 750,000 CAD of holder funds into this placement. A B2B joint development contract was announced in May 2026. The post raised concerns about circular fund flows, insider involvement, and the risk of token holders acting as exit liquidity, urging the community to review financial flows carefully before making long-term investment assumptions.

A community member shared a Twitter post promoting RubyTriviaAI, describing it as an educational app built on the platform with more features coming. The post was shared as an alpha alert for those interested in early-stage projects.

A community member asked about the status of the Hyperforge project, noting it had been quiet. A moderator responded that the team was last heard to be actively working on it over three weeks ago, though current status was uncertain. Separately, a scam warning was issued in the same thread, with multiple users cautioning others not to trust a certain entity flagged as fraudulent.

In the coders channel, a developer posted a job opportunity for a communication support role within a small international development team. The position involves joining client calls and ensuring clear expectations are met. Some technical familiarity is preferred but not required. The role offers light weekly hours with profit-based pay, and interested parties were directed to send a direct message.

Development on June 19, 2026 centered on stabilizing the Eliza Cloud infrastructure following a Neon-to-Railway migration, with key improvements across connection pooling, agent provisioning, performance, and mobile stability.

On the cloud infrastructure side, the team decommissioned BitRouter and transitioned the Cloudflare Worker to serve as the primary model gateway. Round-robin load balancing was implemented for Cerebras API keys, and Cloudflare KV cache support was added to improve reliability. Provisioning workflows were hardened with atomic failure write-backs and self-healing mechanisms.

For agent performance and orchestration, cold-boot times and latency were reduced through the introduction of LEAN_CHAT_PLUGINS and optimized background task handling. The orchestrator was enhanced with sub-agent nesting and MCP auto-inheritance capabilities. A vision plugin issue was also resolved by replacing TensorFlow.js with a native ggml YOLOv8n detector, eliminating build warnings.

On the UI and mobile front, Android and iOS build regressions were fixed, and web-only cloud dashboards were correctly excluded from Capacitor builds. User onboarding was streamlined with auto-skipping of runtime pickers on cloud hosts, and new agent management controls were introduced.

Several items remain in progress, including fixes for orchestrator narration of missing ACP methods, bounding redundant planner loops, SSRF protection for empty addresses, honoring memory limits in the embedding branch, and preserving tool-call content in transcripts.

Two items still require attention: finalizing the transition of the app to the unified proxy and completing the repointing of the provisioning worker's KV store, as well as gathering input on the transition to PgBouncer or Supavisor and database sharding strategies now that connection pooling is live.

In the general discussion channel, community members raised questions and concerns about waifu.fun, a platform associated with the ElizaOS ecosystem. One user questioned the purpose of waifu.fun, noting confusion about who it is intended for and what it does, specifically asking whether it is meant to launch trading agents. Odilitime, a core developer and moderator, acknowledged that he did not build the platform and is still working to understand it himself, but mentioned plans to launch his own agent on it that week to learn more. A separate community member raised a broader concern about token strategy, warning that waifu.fun using its own token rather than ELIZAOS could dilute value and increase market skepticism, urging the team to focus on strengthening one primary token before issuing additional ones. A scammer was also briefly flagged in the channel and was reportedly handled. Additionally, a user inquired about how to contact the team regarding a potential partnership. The coders channel had no activity recorded for the day.

categories.title: ElizaOS Cloud Infrastructure and Managed Agent Improvements (June 20, 2026)

content.text: On June 20, 2026, the ElizaOS development team focused on improving the scalability and reliability of managed agents within the framework. A critical connection pooling issue that caused a 'too many clients' error was resolved by transitioning to a local in-container PGlite database architecture for managed agents. This change significantly improved scalability and stability. Additionally, lean chat plugins were integrated to ensure compatibility with the updated managed agent architecture.

In the general discussion channel, a brief conversation took place between users chulylooly and Odilitime regarding an unidentified topic, with a reference to waifu.fun mentioned as a possible answer. The exchange was inconclusive with chulylooly indicating they had no idea about the subject. Shortly after, the channel was targeted by two separate spam and scam messages. User Levi henry posted a message promoting a rugpull scheme, claiming to have made $24,000 and soliciting others via direct message. User kim6erly followed with an unsolicited job recruitment post advertising paid roles such as beta tester, moderator, developer, and community manager, and used an at-everyone mention to maximize visibility. These messages are consistent with common social engineering and scam tactics seen in crypto and tech community servers. The coders channel had no activity recorded for the same date.

Development on June 21, 2026 focused on framework hardening, UI/UX refinements, and performance optimizations. A mandatory end-to-end ship-gate was established for all plugin routes and commands to ensure comprehensive verification before merging, and UI smoke test specs were updated to align with current product designs for more reliable CI feedback. On the UI/UX front, a new Retry feature was introduced to improve chat reliability, cloud-agent handoffs were made more visible, mobile rendering bugs for the cloud-handoff banner were resolved, and error messaging for rate-limited chat replies was improved. Performance work included optimizations to agent cold-boot times, fixes for sub-agent execution failures, reduced chat latency through parallelized document-recovery searches, and standardized rate-limit detection across the codebase. An urgent open issue requires attention regarding on-device inference, specifically resolving a head_dim incompatibility between 256 and 128 values and addressing non-deterministic races in Mali GPU kernels for Flash Attention. Work in progress includes seeding dedicated-agent personas from default characters, emitting json_object for Cerebras models, refactoring plugin-x auto-posting, CI cleanup to drop duplicate test lanes, batch embedding drain requests, and support for callback functions in character templates.

The ElizaOS community discussed several topics on June 22, 2026. A Ruby Trivia initiative was announced and promoted, with trivia challenges shared publicly on social media covering topics such as Marvel Comics and stock market history. Community members engaged with these posts as part of an ongoing engagement effort.

Questions arose regarding the ElizaOS token and foundation. One community member asked whether the foundation planned to add liquidity for trading ElizaOS on the Solana chain. A core developer responded that they do not communicate directly with the foundation, though they confirmed the foundation manages the token. The identity of foundation members was also inquired about but not answered. Separately, a notable on-chain event was flagged by a community member who observed large token movements from a wallet associated with Shaw, which had been dormant for approximately eight months and moved from 1.02 billion tokens to zero. Other members noted the migration occurred around November or December, roughly seven months prior, and acknowledged the legitimacy of the question given the wallet's long period of inactivity.

A community member asked whether Eliza Cloud or ElizaOS could be expected in 2026. Another member emphasized using the name ElizaOS, describing it as an operating system for AI agents designed to be accessible to everyone. A developer also posted in the community asking if anyone was looking to hire, and another member inquired about the type of development work being sought. A new member introduced themselves as brand new to Eliza and expressed gratitude for being welcomed into the community.
In the coders channel, a single greeting was posted by a community member with no further technical discussion recorded for the day.
